items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The key comic effect is the mock-heroic, high-register phrase «терзают смутные сомнения» applied to petty, absurd “evidence.” The actual translation downgrades this to a casual idiom (“nagging feeling”), losing the theatrical grandiosity. Also, per reference note, «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» here implies each has LOST an item (Shpak’s tape recorder; the ambassador’s medallion), not that they currently possess them. The translation’s “has” misstates the situation and weakens the logic of suspicion.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the core comedic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and awkward self-presentation as “Tsar,” clashing with the historical setting and Bunsha’s drunken, Soviet-formulaic speech. It’s not identical to the reference but achieves the same effect and rhythm well enough for subtitles.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; the pompous demand register is preserved and lands the joke.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Meaning and archaic elevated melancholy are preserved. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than the reference’s more formal cadence, but the line still reads as oddly lofty in context and keeps the humor of misaddressing a younger woman as “old woman.”

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: The core idea (being walled in by “demons”) is conveyed, but the English is clipped and slightly unidiomatic: “Bricked up” reads like a fragment and can evoke unrelated slang. The reference’s “walled me in” better preserves the Tsar’s accusatory, victimized tone and the sense that “demons” are the agents. Comprehension remains, but the comedic/archaic flavor is weakened.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional. It keeps the religious attribution of a mechanical event. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still clearly elevated and humorous in an atheist/Soviet-modern context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly conveyed (a command to dance), but the reference analysis stresses a specific showman/MC formula that heightens the anachronistic “party leader” vibe. “Everybody dance!” is more generic and less quotable; it reduces the performative, announcer-like flourish that makes the moment pop as a catchphrase.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the pragmatic function (self-satisfied delight at a lucky visit) and keeps it light and colloquial. Slightly less “criminally casual” than the reference’s punchy idiom, but still works and remains funny/clear.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; it correctly avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar” in English and preserves the Soviet-satire inversion.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list structure and escalating counting are preserved, including the comedic trailing “—three” for the jacket. “Domestic” is placed naturally (“three domestic cigarette cases”), which is fine. The only slight loss is the breathless, self-correcting rhythm of the Russian/approved ellipses, but the joke remains intact and comprehensible.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Accurate and keeps the calm, rhythmic fatalism. Exclamation marks slightly shift the tone toward sharper comedy/irritation rather than sing-song resignation, but the intended gag (everyone is “mad” and will be treated) still lands.

summary.interpretation: 2.45 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.49 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
